name: Alexander Trautsch

core_identity.summary: The author is an empirical software engineering researcher primarily focused on software quality assurance and defect prediction. Their work critically evaluates and improves existing methods and tools, such as the SZZ algorithm and Automated Static Analysis Tools (ASATs), often through rigorous empirical studies, replication, and benchmarking. They extensively apply and adapt machine learning and Natural Language Processing (NLP) techniques, particularly transformer models, to software engineering contexts like issue type prediction and commit analysis. A consistent theme is the commitment to data quality, validity, and reproducibility in research, often involving large-scale software repository mining, manual validation, and crowd-sourcing for accurate data labeling. The research aims to provide practical insights into improving software development practices and tools.
primary_research_themes: Defect Prediction (especially Cross-Project and Just-in-Time Defect Prediction)
Software Quality Assurance and Improvement (including through automated analysis and prediction)
Empirical Evaluation and Adaptation of Machine Learning/Natural Language Processing (NLP) Techniques in Software Engineering
Critical Evaluation and Improvement of Software Engineering Tools and Practices (e.g., Automated Static Analysis Tools, SZZ algorithm)
Ensuring Data Quality, Validity, and Reproducibility in Software Engineering Research
Leveraging Large-Scale Software Repository Data for Analysis and Prediction
secondary_research_themes: Understanding characteristics of software changes (e.g., tangled commits)
The role of various features and data sources in prediction models
Addressing limitations and inconsistencies in existing research methods and algorithms
The role of open-source projects in empirical software engineering studies
Developing infrastructure and practices for replicable software engineering research (e.g., SmartSHARK)
Challenges and limitations of automated analysis (e.g., false positives in ASATs, distinguishing bug fixes)
The impact of different tool configurations (e.g., ASAT rule sets) on analysis results

methodological_profile.evidence_preferences: Quantitative analysis, statistical evidence, empirical data, measurable outcomes
methodological_profile.research_philosophy: Critical evaluation and improvement, validity, reliability, and reproducibility, problem-solving, establishing clear ground truth
typical_methods: Empirical Studies (including longitudinal, large-scale case studies, and assessments of practice)
Comparative and Quantitative Analysis (including statistical analysis)
Data Collection, Curation, and Annotation (often involving manual labeling, crowd-sourcing, and heuristics)
Software Repository Mining and Commit History Analysis
Machine Learning Model Training and Fine-tuning (especially pre-trained NLP models)
Experimental Evaluation using various performance metrics (e.g., F-measure, G-measure, AUC, MCC, F1-score, recall, precision)

communication_style.voice: Evidence-based, precise, skeptical of unsubstantiated claims

biases: Skepticism towards automated tool outputs (especially regarding false positives/negatives)
Preference for empirical evidence over subjective opinions
Bias towards issues with high defect likelihood

primary_concerns: Likelihood of leading to future defects
Identification of genuinely problematic issues often missed by automated tools
quality_indicators: Context of the code change (commit purpose, size, 'tangledness')
Inferred defect proneness based on change characteristics
Objective and measurable criteria for severity/impact
secondary_concerns: Superficial code style violations (less prioritized unless linked to defects)

annotation_rules: Prioritize issues related to: Likelihood of leading to future defects
Prioritize issues related to: Impact on overall software quality
Prioritize issues related to: Identification of genuinely problematic issues often missed by automated tools

Overall evaluation approach: This researcher would approach code quality evaluation with a highly analytical, data-driven, and critical mindset, prioritizing rigorous, context-aware labeling and a skeptical assessment of automated tool outputs, always seeking to identify issues with a high likelihood of leading to defects.
conceptual_references: Automated Static Analysis Tools (ASATs) and Warnings
Software Repository Mining and Data (Commits, Issue Tracking)
Natural Language Processing (NLP) in Software Engineering
Data Quality, Validity, and Manual Labeling
inference_summary: Alexander Trautsch is a Software Engineering researcher at University of Passau, focused on Defect Prediction (especially Cross-Project and Just-in-Time Defect Prediction), Software Quality Assurance and Improvement (including through automated analysis and prediction), Empirical Evaluation and Adaptation of Machine Learning/Natural Language Processing (NLP) Techniques in Software Engineering. Evaluation approach: This researcher would approach code quality evaluation with a highly analytical, data-driven, and critical mindset, prioritizing rigorous, context-aware labeling and a skeptical assessment of automated tool outputs, always seeking to identify issues with a high likelihood of leading to defects. Communication style: Rigorous, analytical, critical, objective. Known limitations: Awareness of generalizability limitations. Voice: Analytical, Critical, and Evidence-Based
